back: Habits that make people stop listening: gossip, judging, negativity, complaining, excuses, exaggeration, and dogmatism.
The foundation of speech people welcome: Honesty, Authenticity, Integrity, and Love (wishing listeners well).
Grouping ideas in threes makes them sound more compelling and credible, e.g. 'Veni, vidi, vici.'
Repeating an opening clause ('We shall fight...') is the authentic sound of passion and sweeps listeners along.
A balanced sentence makes the thinking behind it seem balanced, so we trust it - even when the balance is an illusion.
Used about once every 16 words; changing only the metaphor can change how people decide. Powerful and easy to abuse.
Classical rhetoric's three levers of persuasion: credibility, emotion, and logic. The best speakers use all three together.
Register (speak from the chest for weight), timbre (warm and rich), prosody, pace, silence, pitch, and volume.

front: Fake it till you become it
back: The finding that confident body language shapes how others see you and how you feel - acting confident builds real confidence.
Protect one big idea, kept short and simple: a tweet-length headline, three messages, and a story behind each.
Visuals push recall from ~10% to ~60% after three days, and people never forget how you made them feel.
Even highly successful people were terrified; repetition builds stronger neural pathways, so speaking gets easier the more you do it.
